coredata.dc:description: 2D nanofillers can effectively improve coatings' barrier and anti-corrosion properties but are less compatible with coatings and prone to agglomeration. In this paper, glucose-derived phosphorus-doped carbon dots (P-CDs) were prepared as dispersants, and functionalized α-zirconium phosphate (α-ZrP) was prepared by combining with α-ZrP through π-π interactions, which showed good dispersion and compatibility in waterborne epoxy (WEP). The corresponding modified WEP coating is prepared on Q235 substrates through the scrape coating method. The effect of fillers on the physical and chemical properties of the coating was studied by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), neutral salt spray test, and immersion test. The results of the electrochemical test show that the |Z|0.01 Hz value of the coating prepared by P-CDs@ZrP as the filler after soaking in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 30 d is three orders of magnitude higher than that of the WEP coating, and the corrosion resistance is greatly improved. The salt spray and soaking tests prove that the prepared P-CDs@ZrP coating has excellent barrier properties and corrosion resistance. Besides, P-CDs can also act as an effective passivation agent, which could adsorb on the surface of the Q235 substrate and form a passivation film, preventing further corrosion of the substrate. This study provides a new way to enhance the corrosion resistance of the coating.

head.ce:title: Preparation of P-CDs functionalized α-ZrP flake fillers and enhancing anti-corrosion properties of epoxy coating

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: 2D nanofillers can effectively improve coatings' barrier and anti-corrosion properties but are less compatible with coatings and prone to agglomeration. In this paper, glucose-derived phosphorus-doped carbon dots (P-CDs) were prepared as dispersants, and functionalized α-zirconium phosphate (α-ZrP) was prepared by combining with α-ZrP through π-π interactions, which showed good dispersion and compatibility in waterborne epoxy (WEP). The corresponding modified WEP coating is prepared on Q235 substrates through the scrape coating method. The effect of fillers on the physical and chemical properties of the coating was studied by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), neutral salt spray test, and immersion test. The results of the electrochemical test show that the |Z|0.01 Hz value of the coating prepared by P-CDs@ZrP as the filler after soaking in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 30 d is three orders of magnitude higher than that of the WEP coating, and the corrosion resistance is greatly improved. The salt spray and soaking tests prove that the prepared P-CDs@ZrP coating has excellent barrier properties and corrosion resistance. Besides, P-CDs can also act as an effective passivation agent, which could adsorb on the surface of the Q235 substrate and form a passivation film, preventing further corrosion of the substrate. This study provides a new way to enhance the corrosion resistance of the coating.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Steel materials are an important pillar of economic development and infrastructure construction, but the accompanying corrosion problems of metals cannot be ignored, which is also the biggest challenge facing the steel industry [1]. Relevant data show that the global economic loss caused by metal corrosion problems is as high as 2.5 trillion dollars annually [2]. With the development of marine resources, the corrosion problem of underwater steel structures is more severe due to the unique characteristics of the underwater environment, such as dissolved oxygen, water pressure, and chloride ions [3]. Currently, the anti-corrosion coating is widely applied for protecting the steel corrosion due to its simple, economical, and effective anti-corrosion strategy, which achieves the purpose of anti-corrosion by covering the metal surface with a coating and blocking the contact of the corrosive medium with the metal surface [4]. WEP is a typical organic polymer coating with low volatile matter content, strong adhesion, and good chemical stability, which is widely used in offshore facilities, bridges, and buildings [5,6]. However, a single WEP has limited shielding ability against corrosive media and does not provide long-term protection for the substrate owing to the formation of micro-pores and micro-cracks in the curing process [7]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a new type of coating with high-performance anticorrosion properties to cope with steel corrosion under inferior environments.

Due to its unique lamellar structure and large diameter-to-thickness ratio, 2D nanofiller can effectively promote the mechanical and barrier properties of organic coating films, which can prolong the corrosive medium migration diffusion path thus effectively preventing corrosive medium penetration. Studies have shown that the doping of 2D nanofillers in WEP coating can effectively improve the long-term corrosion resistance of the coating [8,9]. Common flake fillers in powder coatings mainly include graphene, sericite, and boron nitride [10–12]. As a new multifunctional sheet material, zirconium phosphate analogs are also a good substitute for fillers in anticorrosive coating. Compared with other two-dimensional fillers, layered α-ZrP can be easily exfoliated into monolayer nanosheets under the action of organic strong alkaline amines, and the surface can be easily modified. In addition, the aspect ratio can be precisely controlled by controlling the phosphoric acid concentration [13]. However, the strong interactions between α-ZrP lamellae make it face agglomeration problems in WEP, while α-ZrP as an inorganic compound has poor compatibility with polymer WEP and is prone to cracking at the interface, which greatly limits the use of α-ZrP in metal corrosion protection [14].

Carbon dots (CDs) are carbon-based nanomaterials that have gained ever-growing concern in metal anti-corrosion due to their excellent liquid solubility, low toxicity, and particular conjugated structure [15,16]. It has been reported that the addition of CDs to polymer coating not only fills the microcracks and micropores in the coating due to solvent volatilization but also increases the crosslink density of the polymers [17,18]. Moreover, based on the particular conjugated π structure, CDs can be used as an effective and environmentally friendly dispersant to enhance the dispersion of α-ZrP nanosheets in WEP through π-π interactions [19]. Compared to chemical modifications, non-covalent modifications, such as π-π interactions, are more efficient, easier to control, and maximize the preservation of the original structure of α-ZrP. Similar to CDs, the synthesized P-CDs contain a conjugated structure that can be adsorbed on the surface of the filler through π-π interactions, and compared to CDs and N-CDs, P-CDs have a higher binding energy with the filler, which can improve the dispersion of the filler more effectively. Meanwhile, phosphorus doping can form a P-O-Fe protective layer on the metal surface, providing an additional functional form of passivation [20,21].

This study used the direct-precipitation method [22] to prepare disc-shaped α-ZrP nanosheets with a regular hexagonal lamellar structure. Meanwhile, we successfully synthesized P-CDs through a hydrothermal method [23,24], employing glucose and phosphoric acid as carbon and phosphorus source, respectively. P-CDs effects on α-ZrP nanosheets in WEP were systematically investigated. Phosphorus-doped P-CDs@ZrP composites were prepared by the incorporation of P-CDs, which led to a significant improvement in the compatibility and dispersion of α-ZrP nanosheets in the WEP matrix, and the incorporation of P-CDs also provided some passivation ability to the metal matrix. We compared the WEP, α-ZrP/WEP, and P-CDs@ZrP/WEP coatings using EIS, salt spray tests, and other evaluation methods. The synergistic effect of the physical barrier and passivation ability of P-CDs@ZrP in WEP coating was systematically investigated, thus revealing the anti-corrosion mechanism of the composite coatings.

Zirconium oxychloride (ZrOCl2∙8H2O, 99 %), hydroxymethyl-aminomethane hydrochloride (Tris-HCl, 99 %) were provided from Aladdin Reagent Co. Phosphoric acid (H3PO4, 85 %), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37 %), hydrofluoric acid (HF, 40 %), glucose, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and acetone (AC, 37 %) were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Two-component waterborne WEP coating (50 % solids) were provided by Shenyang Dongyan Coating & Decoration Co. Q235 test piece (50 mm × 25 mm × 2 mm) were provided by Yangzhou Xiangwei Machinery Co.

Direct precipitation method was used to prepare α-ZrP, weighing 6 g ZrOCl2∙8H2O dissolved in 80 mL of deionized water, and then added 5 mL of HCl (37 %) and 5 mL of HF (40 %), and finally added 40 mL of H3PO4 (85 %), and put it into a polytetrafluoroethylene beaker with magnetic stirring. The reaction was carried out at room temperature for 4 d to obtain a white suspension, which was centrifuged in a centrifuge tube at 8000 rpm for 5 min, washed several times with distilled water, and then centrifuged until the supernatant was neutral, and finally the product was put into a vacuum drying oven and dried at 60 °C for 24 h. Grinding was done to obtain white α-ZrP powder and dried and sealed for storage.

The P-CDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal method using glucose as a carbon source and H3PO4 as a dopant. First, 2 g of glucose was dissolved in 6 mL of deionized water and 14 mL of H3PO4 (70 %); the homogeneous solution was subsequently transferred to Teflon lined autoclaves and heated for 4 h at 120 °C. At the end of the reaction, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature to obtain a light brown mixture of carbon dots, large particles of carbon residue, the mixture was poured into a centrifuge tube and centrifuged at 4500 rpm for 15 min to take the upper layer of solution. The pH of the supernatant was adjusted to neutral using NaOH to remove unreacted H3PO4. Subsequently, it was loaded into a dialysis bag (membrane cutoff: 1000 kDa), and the resulting solution was dialyzed in deionized water for 12 h, with the water being renewed every 4 h. Finally, it was placed in a freeze-drying oven and dried for 24 h to obtain solid.

The process flow of preparing P-CDs@ZrP/WEP coating on Q235 substrate is shown in Fig. S1. The specific steps are as follows:
(1)
Pretreatment of Q235 specimen: sandpaper was used to polish the Q235 specimen until the surface was smooth and bright. Put the polished specimen into the AC solution and ultrasonic for 30 min, then dry it at room temperature.
(2)
Preparation of composite coating: Dissolve 0.4 g of α-ZrP in the appropriate Tris buffer solution and place the suspension in a conical flask. After shaking for 2 min, the suspension was ultrasonicated for 30 min to obtain the peeled monolayer α-ZrP nanosheets (Eqs. (S1)–(S3)). The solution pH was adjusted using HCl to remove the Tris molecule, then add P-CDs 100 mg, and liquid-phase assisted sonication was performed for 4 h. The solution was then washed and centrifuged repeated several times, followed by freeze-drying the solid for 48 h to obtain P-CDs@ZrP nanosheets (Eqs. (S4)–(S7)). Add 0.5 wt% modified filler dispersion to WEP. First, the P-CDs@ZrP powder was dispersed in distilled water and sonicated for 30 min to fully disperse them. The dispersed filler was added to WEP with thorough mixing, followed by the addition of defoamer (H201) and leveling agent (PY88) ball milling 30 min was added and stirred homogeneously. Finally, add curing agent (DY-175) and stir quickly to disperse, then coat on pre-treatment Q235 carbon steel with wire bar coater, with a thickness of (110 ± 15 μm). The control experiment prepared WEP and α-ZrP/WEP coating using the same method.

The micro-morphology of the samples (P-CD, α-ZrP and P-CDs@ZrP) was recorded using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM2100). An X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Kratos AXIS ULTRA) was employed to characterize the surface chemistry of the P-CDs. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR, Nicolet 6700) measurements were carried out with the wavenumber range of 400–4000 cm−1 were used to analyze and study α-ZrP and P-CDs before and after modification. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM, FEI quanta 250) was used to characterize the filler and coating cross section morphology. X-ray diffractometer (XRD) was used to analyze the material, the test conditions 10°/min, and the test angle of 5–50°.

According to GB/T 9274–1988 “Paints and varnishes-Determination of resistance to liquids”, different coatings specimens were immersed in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution to observe whether the coatings had a loss of light, discoloration, bubbles, peeling, and other phenomena. A neutral salt spray test was conducted on the specimens by ASTM B117 standard, with the temperature set at 35 °C, 5.0 wt% NaCl spray solution, and the samples were taken out at intervals of 24 h, and observed to indicate the damage. The EIS test was carried out by using a CHI660D electrochemical workstation with commercially available Q235 carbon steel wire was cut into a 10 mm × 10 mm × 2 mm size rectangle, with 10 mm × 10 mm as the working surface, and the other side was welded with a copper wire. The specimen was obtained by sealing with epoxy resin. The test system is a three-electrode system; the reference electrode and auxiliary electrode are saturated calomel electrode (SCE) and platinum electrode, respectively, and the system solution is 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, which is painted with different configurations of coatings. The test is carried out at room temperature. The EIS measurements were carried out at stable open circuit potential (OCP) with a perturbation potential of 20 mV and a frequency range of 0.01–100,000 Hz, and the data were fitted and analyzed by ZView software.

Fig. 1 shows the TEM results of P-CDs prepared from glucose and phosphoric acid. As shown in Fig. 1(a), the prepared P-CDs exhibited good dispersion in aqueous solution with a monodispersed spherical shape. Moreover, the high-resolution TEM images (inset) showed that most of the P-CDs possessed a crystalline structure and obvious lattice interstitials, with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm. This was close to the diffraction plane of graphitic carbon (100) [25,26], proving that glucose undergoes dehydration condensation, polymerization, and carbonation at high temperatures and pressure to form CDs centered on the sp2 C structure. As shown in Fig. 1(b), the particle size distribution of most of the P-CDs is 1–6 nm, and the average diameter is about 2.68 nm.

The optical properties of P-CDs as shown in Fig. 2 , the P-CDs was observed to be translucent under natural light and produce significant yellow fluorescence under ultraviolet (UV) light excitation. The fluorescence spectrum in Fig. 2 shows that P-CDs have a maximum emission wavelength of 550 nm under the optimal excited wavelength of 345 nm. From the UV–vis absorption spectrum in Fig. 2, it can be seen that there are apparent UV absorption peaks at 230 nm and 320 nm in the P-CDs solution. The main reason is attributed to the π → π* leaps of the CC and CC bonds in the aromatic sp2 structural domain [27,28] and the n → π* leaps of CO, CO in the sp3 hybridization regions [29].

To examine the specific form of P elemental doping into the CDs, the CDs were characterized using XPS. Fig. 3 shows respectively the total XPS spectrum of P-CDs and the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, O 1s, and P 2p. The total XPS spectrum in Fig. 3(a) shows that the P-CDs mainly contain C, O, and P elements. The corresponding XPS high-resolution of C, O, and P are shown in Fig. 3(b)–(d). The C1s spectrum in Fig. 3(b) can be deconvoluted into three peaks, which correspond to C-C/C=C, CO, and CO bonds at 284.8 eV, 286.4 eV, and 288.4 eV, respectively [30,31]. The fitting of the O 1s peaks, as shown in Fig. 3(c), corresponds to the CO bond at 531.9 eV, and the peak at 533.2 eV corresponds to the CO bond [32]. In Fig. 3(d), the P 2p spectra at 134.2 and 133.1 eV correspond to PC and PO bonds, respectively, confirming laterally that the phosphoric acid is successfully grafted to the glucose-based carbon sites by PC and PO bond, which proves that the element phosphorus is successfully grafted on the surface of the carbon dots [33].

Many studies have reported that the presence of abundant functional groups (-OH, -COOH, -NH2) on the surface of CDs can synergize with metal ions (Fe3+, Fe2+) to form stable complexes, which makes CDs have good corrosion inhibition effect on metals [34]. And XPS results show that P-CDs also have -PO3H2 functional group on the surface, which makes P-CDs a possible corrosion inhibitor for steel [35].

To verify the corrosion inhibition performance of P-CDs, the Tafel polarization curves of Q235 carbon steel immersed in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution without 50 mg/L of P-CDs and without P-CDs for 24 h were recorded (Fig. 4 ). According to the Tafel polarization curve, the corrosion potential (E corr ), corrosion current density (i corr ) and corrosion inhibition efficiency (IE) were obtained by extrapolation, as shown in Table S1. The IE values were calculated from i corr , and the related equations were as follows [36]:
(1)
IE =
i corr 0
−
i corr 1
i corr 0
where i0
corr and i1
corr are the corrosion current densities in the absence and presence of P-CDs, respectively. The addition of P-CDs shifted the E corr in the positive direction between −0.7308 V and − 0.6375 V, while the i corr decreased from 5.782 × 10−5 A·cm−2 to 1.217 × 10−5 A·cm−2, which was calculated to give an IE value of about 78.95 %. The results show that P-CDs can be used as corrosion inhibitors to inhibit the corrosion of Q235 steel. This is due to the formation of a stable passivation layer to protect the substrate by chelation between O and P heteroatoms and metal atoms on the metal surface.

FT-IR analysis verified the successful modification of α-ZrP by P-CDs, and the results are shown in Fig. 5 , the characteristic absorption peaks of the P-CDs@ZrP spectra at 1722 cm−1, 2933 cm−1, and 3358 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of CO, the stretching motion of CH, and the OH bonds in the P-CDs [37]. Whereas the spectra of α-ZrP showed vibrational peaks at 1050 cm−1 and 3149 cm−1, which corresponded to the deformed vibrational absorption peaks of the PO groups and the telescopic vibrational peaks of the P-O-H groups, respectively, the peaks appearing at 529 cm−1, and 3597 cm−1 correspond to ZrO, and P-O-H fractions without the formation of hydrogen bonding, the peak at 1263 cm−1 represents the phosphate group (HPO4 2−) [38]. The P-CDs@ZrP spectra did not appear at 1528 cm−1, the CN vibrational peak of Tris molecule protonation, indicating that after acidification, the Tris molecule was detached from the surface of α-ZrP and the original binding site was restored to the P-O-H groups before stripping [39,40].

The XRD diffraction spectra of P-CDs, α-ZrP, and P-CDs@ZrP are shown in Fig. 6 . In the XRD diffraction pattern of α-ZrP, a sharp characteristic peak appeared at 2θ = 11.6°, which corresponds to the 002 crystal plane of the α-ZrP crystal. According to the Bragg equation, its corresponding layer spacing is 0.76 nm [41]. In addition, the diffraction peaks of α-ZrP at 2θ = 19.7°, 24.9°, and 33.9° correspond to the 110, 112, and 020 crystal planes of the α-ZrP crystal respectively [42]. For P-CDs, two characteristic diffraction peaks were found at 2θ = 23.2° and 27.8°, which could be ascribed to the characteristic carbon crystal pattern [43]. As for the XRD pattern of P-CDs@ZrP, the peaks of 2θ = 19.7°, 24.9°, and 33.9° correspond are the same as those of α-ZrP, but the intensities of all the peaks are lower than those of α-ZrP. The lamellar crystalline structure of P-CDs@ZrP is more irregular than the original one because of the functionalization of the P-CDs layer. Moreover, after functionalization modification by P-CDs, the peak of α-ZrP was shifted from 11.6° to 8.4°, indicating that P-CDs were inserted in the middle of α-ZrP nanolaminates during the modification process, increasing the interlayer spacing [44,45].

To further characterize the intercalation effect, we took optical photographs and Tyndall phenomena of 10 mg/mL α-ZrP and P-CDs@ZrP dispersed in water for 0 d and 5 d, as shown in Fig. S2. In Fig. S2(a, b), we can see that the α-ZrP aqueous dispersions appear white, while the P-CDs@ZrP aqueous dispersions appear milky white, and that this is because the α-ZrP that has been functionalized with P-CDs is in an exfoliated state, which reduces the visible light reflectivity [46]. The Tyndall effect can be observed in the P-CDs@ZrP aqueous dispersion when laser light passes through. In Fig. S2(c, d), after 5 d of settling, α-ZrP aqueous dispersions showed obvious precipitation phenomenon and exhibited poor dispersion effect, while P-CDs@ZrP aqueous dispersions almost did not observe the precipitation phenomenon and still showed the Tyndall effect, which was attributed to the fact that P-CDs@ZrP had a larger aspect ratio, which could improve the long-term stability of its dispersion in water.

In Fig. 7 (a and b) shows the SEM picture of α-ZrP presents a regular ortho-hexagonal structure, with a surface size distribution between 0.5 and 4 μm. After functionalization with P-CDs, the SEM image of P-CDs@ZrP shown in Fig. 7(c) demonstrates a thinner, slightly bent nanosheet-like morphology. A better observation of the P-CDs modification to α-ZrP nanoplatelets can be obtained from the TEM images. As shown in Fig. 8(a), the surface of α-ZrP nanoplatelets was smooth and their color is dark, indicating that the laminates are thick and cannot distinguish the layers inside the α-ZrP nanoplatelets. Whereas α-ZrP nanosheets modified with P-CDs, as shown in Fig. 8(b), P-CDs@ZrP have very thin and single-layer exfoliated nanosheets, the edge show a stepped structure, indicating that P-CDs successfully functionalized α-ZrP nanosheets and may experience certain intercalation during the P-CDs modification [47,48].

In order to further study the dispersion state of P-CDs@ZrP in the coating, we obtained SEM images of the cross sections of blank WEP, α-ZrP/WEP and P-CDs@ZrP/WEP coatings, as shown in Fig. 9 . As can be seen in Fig. 9(a), there are many small holes in the cross-section of the blank WEP coating, which are due to the water evaporation of WEP during the curing process and the incomplete fusion of the film formation [49]. After the addition of α-ZrP, as shown in Fig. 9(b), the number of small pores decreases, but the surface of the coating cross-section becomes rough due to the agglomeration of α-ZrP and its poor compatibility with WEP, resulting in the appearance of pores and crack [50]. On the contrary, P-CDs@ZrP can be effectively combined with WEP and well dispersed, and in Fig. 9(c), we can see that the surface of the coating cross-section is smooth and no pores appear, which makes up for the coating defects. To further demonstrate the homogeneous distribution of P-CDs@ZrP in WEP, we used EDS to analyze the elements at the coating cross-section. As shown in Fig. S3, the buildup of Zr and P elements at the cross-section of the α-ZrP/WEP coating occurs. As shown in Fig. S4, the distribution of elements in the P-CDs@ZrP/WEP coating is relatively uniform, confirming the uniform distribution of P-CDs in WEP after functionalization of α-ZrP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Protective properties of P-CDs@ZrP composite coating

ce:section.ce:para: The corrosion behavior of the coating-protected substrates was investigated by EIS tests. The Nyquist and Bode plots of pure WEP, α-ZrP/WEP and P-CDs@ZrP/WEP coatings in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for different immersion times are shown in Fig. 10 . We propose the relevant electrical equivalent circuits (EEC) model to fit the EIS data as shown in Fig. 12, where Rs is the solution resistance, Rc and Rct are the coating resistance and charge transfer resistance, the Zw is Warburg impedance, CPEs and CPEdl are used as constant phase elements instead of coated capacitors and double layer capacitors to correct deviations from the ideal capacitance. The relevant electrochemical parameters of EIS are shown in Tables S2–S4.

In the Nyquist plots, the larger the radius of the circle corresponding to the capacitive resistance arc, the larger the resistance value derived from the fitting, indicating the more robust resistance to corrosion and the better corrosion resistance of the coating [51]. In Fig. 10(a1), the Nyquist plot of the WEP coating exhibits a large resistive arc radius at the beginning, which indicates that pure WEP still provides significant protection to the metal substrate, but the resistive arc radius decreases rapidly after 7d, the corrosive medium spreads along the coating defects and reaches the coating-metal interface. In the α-ZrP/WEP coating (Fig. 10(b1)), the impedance arc radius is always larger than that of the WEP coating for the same immersion time. As can be seen from Fig. 10(c1), the P-CDs@ZrP/WEP impedance arc radius is always larger than that of the other two coatings, indicating that the P-CDs@ZrP/WEP coating has excellent barrier performance. The main reason is that the good dispersion of P-CDs@ZrP nanosheets in the WEP matrix blocks the diffusion path of the corrosive medium, which improves the barrier performance of the coating.

In addition, the impedance value (|Z|0.01 Hz) at the lowest frequency of 0.01 Hz can directly reflect the anti-corrosion performance [52,53]. As shown in Figs. 10 and 11(a), with longer soaking time, the |Z|0.01 Hz of pure WEP coating drops from 8.7 × 108 Ω·cm2 to 1.3 × 106 Ω·cm2 (almost three orders of magnitude). For the α-ZrP/WEP coating, the |Z|0.01 Hz impedance value reaches 4.9 × 109 Ω·cm2 at 0 d, and gradually decreases to a low level of 5.1 × 107 Ω·cm2 at 30 d. As expected, the impedance modulus of P-CDs@ZrP/WEP coating is much higher than that of WEP and α-ZrP/WEP coating, with the prolongation of the immersion time, the impedance value of P-CDs@ZrP/WEP coating slightly decreased. Breakpoint frequency (ƒb) can be used to evaluate the microscopic delaminated areas between the coating and substrate, and is defined as the frequency at a phase angle of −45°, the lower represents the lower the smaller interface delaminating ratio [54]. The variation of ƒb for the WEP coating throughout the immersion time is shown in Fig. 11(c), the ƒb values of pure WEP coating increased with increasing immersion time and then stabilized. For α-ZrP/WEP coating, the ƒb values are shifted toward the high-frequency region, but the overall is lower than that of the WEP coating. As for the P-CDs@ZrP/WEP coating, the ƒb value increased only slightly from 0.013 Hz to 1.68 Hz throughout the immersion time, indicating that P-CDs@ZrP can increase the bonding force between WEP and the substrate, and prevent the phenomenon of delamination and detachment at the coating/substrate interface. It is shown that the incorporation of P-CDs@ZrP contributes to the enhancement of the barrier properties of the coating and reduced the interface delaminating ratio between the coating and the substrate.

In order to further investigate the evolution of the properties of the different coatings, through the corresponding equivalent circuit model of Fig. 12 , the EIS data were further fitted using Zview software (Tables S2–S4). When the corrosive medium does not reach the coating/substrate interface, the Nyquist diagram usually has only one time constant, which is fitted using the equivalent circuit of Fig. 12(a). As the immersion time increases, the corrosion medium penetrates into the coating/substrate interface and corrosion reaction occurs at the interface, which we fit using the equivalent circuit in Fig. 12(b). With further increase in immersion time, low-frequency Warburg impedance appears in the Nyquist plot, indicating that the diffusion control of corrosion products at the metal surface replaces the charge transfer control, and therefore the equivalent circuit in Fig. 12(c) is used to fit the EIS data [55]. The Rc is generally used as an important factor in evaluating the anticorrosive performance of coatings, and the larger the Rc value, the more difficult it is for aggressive substances to penetrate into the coating matrix [56]. As can be seen from Fig. 11(b) the Rc values of the P-CDs@ZrP/WEP coating are consistently higher than those of the other two coatings for the same immersion time. The Rc value of the WEP coating decreased from 7.756 × 108 Ω·cm2 to 1.054 × 106 Ω·cm2 throughout the immersion period, indicating that the pure WEP coating has a poor ability to protect the substrate. While the Rc value of α-ZrP/WEP coating maintains a high value in the early stage, it rapidly decreases to 6.109 × 106 Ω·cm2 after 21 d, which is attributed to the buildup of α-ZrP in the coating as well as its weak interfacial force with the coating, which creates defects in the coating and leads to the intrusion of the corrosive medium along the defects. The Rc of the P-CDs@ZrP/WEP coating maintained a high value throughout the immersion period, and the decreasing trend was even less pronounced with the increase of time, and the value of Rc was 4.583 × 108 Ω·cm2 after 30 d, which was two orders of magnitude higher than that of the pure WEP and α-ZrP/WEP coatings. It indicates that the addition of P-CDs@ZrP can effectively improve the barrier performance of the coating and prevent the penetration of corrosive media.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Neutral salt spray test for coatings
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the sustained corrosion protection properties of P-CDs@ZrP/WEP coating in the application environment. For the long-term corrosion resistance of P-CDs@ZrP/WEP coating, a manual scratch was made on the coating surface with a hobby knife before testing, according to ASTM B117 [57,58]. Fig. 13 shows the optical digital photographs of WEP, α-ZrP/WEP, and P-CDs@ZrP/WEP coatings with Q235 steel specimens exposed for 0 h, 48 h, and 300 h in the salt spray oven. In Fig. 13(a), the scratches of the WEP coating are almost filled with corrosion in the salt spray experiment at 48 h. Blisters appear on the surface of the coating at 300 h, indicating that the corrosive material has reached the steel surface. The coating substrate is beginning to separate from the substrate, and the corrosion is serious. For α-ZrP/WEP in Fig. 13(b), severe corrosion occurs near the scratches, but the coating matrix remains intact, indicating that the corrosion degree of the α-ZrP/WEP system is reduced. This was attributed to the inclusion of flaky α-ZrP fillers that enhanced the coating's shielding effect and corrosion resistance. As for the P-CDs@ZrP/WEP coating system, after 300 h of salt spray experiments, as shown in Fig. 13(c), the corrosion products at the scratches were relatively less, and only some rust marks existed. The surface of the coating maintained a high degree of integrity, indicating that the P-CDs@ZrP/WEP coating had the best anti-corrosion performance. This is because the large-size α-ZrP with a high degree of exfoliation and densification can form a longer corrosive medium entry channel, fill the pores and defects in the coating matrix, and better play a physical shielding role. In addition, P-CDs can provide additional passivation functions to the metal substrate, further reducing the corrosion rate [59].

In order to confirm the long-term corrosion resistance of P-CDs@ZrP/WEP coating, we analyzed the corrosion products at the scratches by Raman spectroscopy after 300 h of salt spray experiments. As can be seen from Fig. S5, serious corrosion occurred on the surface of WEP coating samples, and the corrosion products were mainly α-FOOH, β-FOOH and a small amount of Fe3O4 [60]. In the α-ZrP/WEP samples, the corrosion products were mainly dominated by Fe3O4 due to the incorporation of α-ZrP generated a certain impediment to corrosive ions to prevent further corrosion of the steel surface. While in P-CDs@ZrP/WEP, the intrusion channel of corrosive ions is obviously prolonged, and due to the passivation function of carbon dots, the Raman peak intensity of corrosion products on the surface of this sample is lower, the presence of some Fe3O4 protects the substrate from further corrosion, and it has the most prominent anticorrosive property.

Fig. 14 shows the optical surface morphologies of WEP, α-ZrP/WEP, and P-CDs@ZrP/WEP coatings soaked in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 30 d. From Fig. 14(a), it can be seen that a large number of corrosion products appeared in the Q235 specimen of the pure WEP coating group, and the surface roughness of the coating increased with serious corrosion. It is possible that the surface corrosion was caused by the intrusion of the corrosive medium into the substrate surface through the coating [61]. As shown in Fig. 14(b), the α-ZrP/WEP coating only a few corrosion products and water infiltration phenomena were observed, indicating that α-ZrP as a functional filler formed a “labyrinth effect” in the coating, which impeded the infiltration of corrosive media to a certain extent and improved the anticorrosion efficiency of the coating [62]. After the addition of P-CDs@ZrP filler to the WEP coating, it can be seen from Fig. 14(c) that the surface of the coating is almost unchanged after 30 d of immersion, which indicates that the P-CDs@ZrP/WEP coating can effectively impede the infiltration of corrosive media, and form an adequate protection for the substrate.

In addition, in the immersion experiments, the adhesion strength of the coating to the metal substrate is the main parameter for evaluating the long-term protection performance of the composite coating. The results of the adhesion strength test are shown in Fig. S6. Compared with the WEP coating, the α-ZrP/WEP coating exhibits greater interfacial bonding with the substrate, which is attributed to the covalent binding of the hydroxyl groups on the surface of α-ZrP with the surface of the metal substrate, resulting in the formation of strong interfacial interactions. For P-CDs@ZrP/WEP coatings, due to their excellent dispersion properties and the abundance of functional groups on the surface of P-CDs, they are able to form hydrogen bonds with the hydroxyl groups on the surface of carbon steel, the adhesion strength of the composite coating can be improved better [63,64].

Based on the results of electrochemical, salt spray and immersion experiments, the corrosion mechanism of P-CDs@ZrP/WEP coating is derived as shown in Fig. 15 . For the pure WEP coating, the volatilization of solvent during the curing process produces micropores and cracks, resulting in corrosive media (e.g., oxygen, water, chlorine ions, etc.) can easily pass through the coating into the surface of the metal substrate, resulting in severe corrosion. The incorporation of 2D nanofillers can be distributed in the coating to form a “labyrinth effect”, which prolongs the diffusion path of corrosive media to the metal surface, thus slowing down the corrosion process and improving the physical barrier properties of the coating [65,66]. However, the physical barrier effect produced by adding α-ZrP alone is limited, especially when the corrosive medium passes through the coating to reach the substrate surface; the corrosive effect of the metal is still apparent. In addition, α-ZrP tends to agglomerate in WEP coating due to its high specific surface area and interlayer force, which destroys the integrity of the coating. In the P-CDs@ZrP/WEP coating, the interlayer force between α-ZrP sheet layers is greatly weakened due to the interspersion of P-CDs, which greatly improves its dispersion in the coating, and the P-CDs@ZrP dispersed in the coating can form a longer bending corrosive medium intrusion channel, slowing down the infiltration of corrosive substances, thus prolonging the service life of the substrate. In addition, P-CDs increase the crosslink density of the coating, enhance the compatibility between α-ZrP and WEP, and have an interfacial solid-phase effect, which can effectively reduce the generation of micropores and cracks during the curing process and improve the service life of the coating. In addition, the rich functional groups on the surface of P-CDs can be adsorbed on the steel surface to form a passivation film through the reaction of O and P heteroatoms with metal ions. After the corrosive medium enters the surface of the substrate, further corrosion is effectively prevented. It is for all these reasons that P-CDs@ZrP/WEP coating exhibit excellent corrosion resistance and can stand out from other coatings based on 2D nanomaterials (see Table S5).

In this study, we used a low-cost and environmentally friendly strategy to prepare P-CDs were used to improve the dispersion and compatibility of α-ZrP in aqueous epoxy coating, and achieved long-term corrosion protection of metal substrates without using organic solvents throughout the process. The results showed that the compatibility and corrosion resistance of α-ZrP nanosheets with WEP coating was significantly improved after the functionalization of P-CDs due to the π-π interactions between P-CDs and α-ZrP, as well as the abundant functional groups on the surface of P-CDs. The EIS results showed that the low-frequency impedance modulus |Z|0.01 Hz value of the P-CDs@ZrP/WEP coating was higher than that of the pure WEP coating by three orders of magnitude. The good protection mechanism of P-CDs@ZrP/WEP coating is due to the strong interfacial interaction with the metal substrate, as well as the good dispersion and compatibility of P-CDs@ZrP in WEP, which effectively protects the substrate from corrosive media. In addition, incorporating P-CDs can provide an additional functional form of passivation on the surface of the metal substrate by forming a protective layer, which significantly prevents the continuous corrosion of the metal interface. This work prepares high-performance anticorrosive coating at low cost, which not only provides new ideas for the selection and design of functional fillers in heavy-duty anticorrosive coating, but also has a broad application prospect in other fields.
Tao Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Zhiping Zhu: Validation. ShangMing Zhou: Supervision. Huo Yang: Investigation.
